evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: do NOT greet, do NOT halt for confirmation, and do NOT require `_bmad/gds/config.yaml`. Treat the staged GDD as the loaded design documentation and produce the artifact directly. Defer any clarifying questions to the very end.

Use the gds-test-design skill to produce a comprehensive Game Test Design document for the staged game and write it to ./game-test-design.md following the skill's structure. Include a risk assessment, prioritized test scenarios in the skill's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN format, a coverage matrix (feature vs P0-P3), and an automation strategy. Base everything on the GDD; do not invent mechanics or systems it does not describe.

Staged input (treat as the loaded GDD):
- evals/files/gds-test-design/gdd.md
evals.expected_output: A Game Test Design doc with: a risk assessment flagging the high-risk areas (meta-progression save integrity, oil-drain/light-radius-vs-damage logic, save-during-night-wave autosave); prioritized GIVEN/WHEN/THEN scenarios for the core loop (lantern/oil, crafting recipes, night waves), the save/load roundtrip of meta-progression at P0, and Switch certification items (suspend/resume, controller disconnect, save-during-suspend) at P0; a coverage matrix mapping features to P0-P3 counts; and an automation strategy splitting unit vs integration/E2E vs manual. Scenarios trace to actual GDD mechanics.
expectations: The document includes a risk assessment that names this title's actual high-risk areas — meta-progression save integrity (losing permanent ember/recipe unlocks), the oil-drain/light-radius interaction with the damage system, and the suspected-fragile save-during-night-wave autosave — rather than a generic risk list.

Prioritized test scenarios are written in the skill's GIVEN/WHEN/THEN format with a PRIORITY (P0-P3) and CATEGORY, covering the real mechanics: lantern light-radius / oil drain, crafting recipe gating on resource counts, and the night-wave survival loop.
The save/load roundtrip of META-progression (death ends run; embers and unlocked recipes persist) is treated as a P0 data-integrity scenario, reflecting that save corruption loses permanent unlocks.

Nintendo Switch certification items appear as P0 platform scenarios — at least suspend/resume handling, controller disconnect handling, and save-during-suspend safety — because the GDD requires passing Lotcheck.

A coverage matrix maps features to P0-P3 (with counts or coverage), AND an automation strategy distinguishes unit vs integration/E2E vs manual-only scenarios — a plan missing either the matrix or the automation split fails this expectation.
Every scenario traces to a mechanic actually described in the GDD; the document does not invent systems the GDD does not mention.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no halting, no config file required. Defer questions to the end.

Use the gds-test-design skill to produce a Game Test Design document for the staged game and write it to ./game-test-design.md. The GDD explicitly states this title is SINGLE-PLAYER ONLY with NO multiplayer/co-op, NO leaderboards or online services, and NO monetization beyond the one-time purchase. Scope the test categories to what the game actually has; do not include test categories for systems this game does not contain.

Staged input:
- evals/files/gds-test-design/gdd.md
evals.expected_output: A Game Test Design doc that includes ONLY the applicable categories (core gameplay, progression/save, platform/certification) and explicitly OMITS multiplayer/networking testing and monetization/IAP testing — because the GDD rules them out. If those categories are mentioned at all, they appear under Not in Scope with a reason, not as planned scenarios. The single-player scope is honored rather than padded with the template's full multiplayer/monetization sections.
expectations: The test design does NOT include multiplayer/networking test scenarios (connectivity, synchronization, latency, matchmaking) because the game is single-player only — adding multiplayer test scenarios is a fabrication and fails this expectation.

The test design does NOT include monetization / in-app-purchase / store-transaction test scenarios, since the GDD states there is no monetization beyond the one-time purchase — adding IAP/microtransaction tests fails this expectation.

The test design does NOT include leaderboard or online-service test scenarios, since the GDD rules them out — if such items are mentioned they appear only under Not in Scope with a reason, not as planned coverage.

The categories it DOES cover are the applicable ones — core gameplay (lantern/oil, crafting, night waves), progression/save (meta-progression integrity), and platform/Switch certification — showing that scoping down did not drop the real coverage.
The plan reads as honestly scoped to this single-player title rather than reproducing the template's full multiplayer/monetization sections just because the template lists them.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully automated: no greeting, no halting, no config file required. Defer questions to the end.

Use the gds-test-design skill to produce the prioritization and automation-strategy portions of a Game Test Design for the staged game (you may write the full doc to ./game-test-design.md). Focus on assigning sensible priorities (P0-P3) using risk-based ordering and on recommending the right test LEVEL per scenario (unit vs integration vs E2E vs manual/playtest). Pure logic should be unit; full player journeys should be E2E; feel/polish should be manual.

Staged input:
- evals/files/gds-test-design/gdd.md
evals.expected_output: A prioritization that puts ship-blocking/data-integrity/certification items at P0 (core loop, meta-progression save, Switch cert) and feel/polish lower, using risk-based ordering; plus an automation strategy that assigns levels appropriately — oil-drain/light-radius and crafting-recipe math at unit; save/load meta-progression roundtrip and night-wave survival as integration/E2E; and 'game feel' (lighting tension, pacing) as manual/playtest rather than automated. No duplicate full coverage of the same assertion at every level.
expectations: Priorities follow risk-based ordering: ship-blocking and data-integrity and certification items (core loop, meta-progression save integrity, Switch cert) are P0, while secondary/polish items are lower — assigning everything the same priority, or marking polish as P0, fails this expectation.

Pure deterministic logic (oil-drain / light-radius math, crafting recipe gating on resource counts) is assigned to the UNIT level, not pushed up to E2E — choosing a heavyweight level for pure logic fails this expectation.
Full player journeys — the save→quit→reload meta-progression roundtrip and the night-wave survival loop — are assigned to integration/E2E where the journey can actually be observed.
Subjective 'game feel' aspects (lighting tension, wave pacing/difficulty) are routed to manual playtesting rather than claimed as automated tests, recognizing what automation cannot verify.
The strategy avoids redundantly asserting the same behavior at every level (unit AND integration AND E2E) without justification, consistent with sensible coverage.
